data.title: AT&T: Building The Right Way
data.description:
AT&T (T) has received a whole lot of criticism for the original shopping spree that not only got it into cellular but also brought a lot of problems it spent the last few years solving. The fact is that the company made it from a landline company to what it is today. That alone is something many companies in declining industries do not accomplish. Just look at the steel industry, for example. Not only is the company I worked for, Bethlehem Steel, not around, but virtually the whole industry was replaced by brand-new companies like Nucor (NUE). Even though I was told I would never need another job, the future was very different. AT&T deserves a lot of credit for getting to the present in a viable form. It could have done better. But it did well just to survive to become a viable investment option again.

Acquisition Announcement

Now, the latest acquisition announcement brought a whole lot of cries of "not again". But the acquisition of the business from Lumen (LUMN) is very different from past mistakes. Rather than "proving" that a company never learns from the past, this acquisition very much shows that the company did learn from its past to build upon the future.

Michael Porter covers this in his book "Competitive Strategies" for those who want more details. David Dreman covers this from an investing viewpoint in his "Contrarian Investment" book series.

Unlike the past acquisitions that caused a lot of issues and investor pain, this acquisition expands the current business that the company knows very well. Furthermore, the acquisition is relatively small at less than $6 billion in cash. The acquisition is also focused on the fiber business of AT&T. This is a business that the company wants to grow.

That means that the company is not expanding into a new business. Rather, the company is expanding a business it knows well into new areas. Management has also announced that they will joint venture in this area so that investors will still see guidance maintained.

That rather smart joint venture announcement keeps the dividend on solid ground. Otherwise, there is a chance that the company would have to cut the dividend to integrate these operations while "bringing them up to acceptable standards". Given the outcry some years back when the dividend was cut, the joint venture was the obvious option forward. The deal remains accretive while investors are not upset.

Furthermore, relatively small acquisitions in areas well known by management have a far better history of success. Remember that the shopping spree of the past got this company into new areas with each acquisition. Furthermore, some of those acquisitions were not well run to begin with. Turning around such a situation is "easier said than done" as Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) just demonstrated. This acquisition eliminated the rather fraught pathway (thank goodness).

Guidance

Fiscal year 2025 guidance was reinforced. Remember that with all the divestments, downsizing, and refocusing, the company has a relatively new cash pattern where the first quarter was the weakest, and the fourth quarter will likely be the most significant. Some competitors have had this pattern for a while. But this newly refocused company had other businesses that clouded the cash flow picture that exists now.

What management is doing is reinforcing that the new cash pattern will hold again this year because the company is "not going anywhere". Instead, it intends to do what it does best while adding small deals as they become available.

But since the company is already a dominant force in the fiber industry, the rollup type deals like the one that just happened will likely be few and far between. But they could potentially add a percent or maybe two percent to the long-term growth rate.

Last Article

The last article noted that the company posted free cash flow in the first quarter for the second time since all targeted the divestments completed. This is the company's weakest cash flow quarter. So positive free cash flow in the first quarter bodes well for the rest of the fiscal year.

What should be becoming apparent to investors is that the debt ratio and free cash flow make this company far healthier than it was. To this day, it surprises me that the debt rating agencies did not at least issue a warning about the situation. Then again, management clearly solved the problem on its own.

This brings to the discussion that one of the characteristics of investment grade debt rated companies is that they generally resolve their issues before the public realizes that things have gotten past serious. The only real surprise was the lack of focus on cash that Warner Bros Discovery found after they got control of the acquired assets. It may be why there is a relatively new CFO here at AT&T.

Summary

The current management is proving to be quite a bit more agile and flexible than some managements of the past. The announcement of a small acquisition is a case in point for those pointing to much tighter management control. Clearly, in my opinion, this management is not about to repeat the mistakes of the past.

The case remains for a strong buy recommendation as a growth and income idea. Investors can continue to expect decent single digit business growth combined with a dividend yield to see a total return likely in the low teens over time from the current price.

There was a recovery potential that has since been essentially realized. That is replaced with the idea that this stock is likely to be recession resistant. In fact, earnings are likely to continue to grow through any recession. This is likely to be one of the last companies affected by a business downturn.

Risks

There is a complacency risk with a big company like this that competes in an oligarchy where competition is not what it is in a highly fractured industry. As a result, large companies like this one are often forever on the "comeback trail" because they get complacent every time the comeback is finished. It seems like the next CEO forever takes "easy street" which puts the CEO after that on cleanup duty.

This latest acquisition has a very low chance of failure. The risk is that management goes on another shopping spree, as it did in the past that got the company into a bunch of businesses it had no business even looking at in the first place. Sometimes "shopping sprees" go to management's heads, as if they can do "nothing wrong" until the next management details the mess and cleanup strategy.

data.title: Mako Mining: Operating One Of The World's Highest Grade Open-Pit Mines
data.description:
Summary

At the request of a long-time follower, I continue my research within the precious metals mining industry with Mako Mining (TSXV:MKO:CA) (OTCQX:MAKOF), a small gold producer operating the high-grade San Albino open-pit mine in Nicaragua. Given its low operating costs, the free cash flows coming from San Albino can then be reinvested in growth opportunities, either through the drill bit or inorganically. I view Mako's self-funding business model as interesting, and I must admit that I am quite impressed by the company's tight control over executive compensation and administrative costs. As illustrated by the acquisition of the Moss Mine for a net cash acquisition cost of ~$2 million, management appears to manage capital quite efficiently.

However, before discussing Mako in more detail, I would like to say a few words on the geopolitical developments of the last few days, given their important implication for precious metals prices. It is my personal opinion that today's events in the Middle East could mark the beginning of something much larger and consequential than what most people expect.

Middle East Geopolitics

I am writing these lines as oil prices are spiking by almost 8% after Israel conducted military strikes on Iran. As discussed in a previous article about the containership leasing company Danaos Corporation (DAC) in November 2024, a hot war between Israel and Iran could have serious implications for the Strait of Hormuz, the jugular vein of the world economy. According to various news outlets, Iran's armed forces are already considering the closure of Hormuz. Without a surprise, the probability of such an event has increased sharply on the Polymarket betting platform. What was considered a tail risk a few months ago is now becoming front and center.

The deteriorating conditions in the Middle East are slowly putting the Strait of Hormuz into focus. Even though this route is absolutely vital for oil trade, it is less so for containership. In my view, the real deal when it comes to geopolitical risk is the Taiwan Strait, and if Hormuz is the world's jugular vein for oil, the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea are the jugulars for containerships. Bujon Capital - November 2024

Over the years, Iran has always been clear that in the event of a direct conflict with Israel, it would shut down maritime traffic in the Strait of Hormuz. As a reminder, approximately 20% of the world's oil consumption flows through this narrow passage, and we can all imagine the potential impacts of its closure on oil prices. The entire globe would be subject to another wave of inflation, potentially larger than the one we have all experienced following the lockdowns of March 2020.

In turn, increasing inflation expectations may lead to additional selling pressure on the Treasury market. After all, the United States is already running massive fiscal deficits, and another war in the Middle East would only make the problem worse. Interest expenses are rapidly compounding, and the feedback loop of debt issuances is becoming increasingly obvious. The United States and many other Western countries are finding themselves in a debt trap, so why would investors seek safety in their sovereign bonds?

More and more people are starting to ask those hard questions, and once critical mass is reached, their herding instinct could fuel a historic bull market in precious metals. After all, precious metals held in physical form are one of the few asset classes without counterparty risks. After reading the book 'The Great Taking' by David Rogers Webb, I cannot say the same about paper gold traded on financial markets, unfortunately. Clearing houses may become the focal point during the next financial crisis, and this is precisely when the notion of counterparty risks could come back with a vengeance in the public's consciousness.

For reasons highlighted in the research paper 'Resilience redux in the US Treasury market' published in August 2023, the Federal Reserve would be forced to intervene in case of a severe dislocation in the Treasury market. Ultimately, ensuring market stability in their respective sovereign bond markets is one of the primary functions of central banks. After another round of money printing, what would happen to precious metal prices? Given the record amount of debt outstanding, the required bailout would have to be larger than the previous one. This is how the current monetary system operates: akin to a worldwide Ponzi scheme. By focusing my research efforts on the precious metals mining industry, my hope is to be at the forefront of what could be a historic bull market. It is from this geopolitical and macroeconomic standpoint that I approach Mako Mining.

High-Grade Mining In Nicaragua

Mako's flagship asset is the high-grade San Albino mine in Nicaragua. With a measured and indicated resource grade averaging 11.61 g/t, San Albino is one of the world's highest grade open-pit mines. In the first quarter of 2025, Mako milled ore with an average grade of 7.1 g/t, but that includes dilution from lower-grade zones. It is quite interesting that I am referring to an open-pit gold grade of almost 3 g/t as 'lower grade'. Of course, this is only true when compared to ore grading in excess of 10 g/t from diluted veins.

When I think about high-grade open-pit mines, the first name that comes to my mind is B2Gold's (BTG) Goose Project in Nunavut. As discussed in a previous article about the company in November 2024, Goose is estimated to host ~6.3 million ounces of gold at an average grade of 5.88 g/t, which compares to San Albino's ~250,000 ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 11.61 g/t. In addition, production volumes at Goose are expected to surpass 300,000 ounces per year in the coming year, while San Albino produced just short of 40,000 ounces in 2024. Finally, Goose is located in one of the harshest and most remote places on Earth, while San Albino benefits from a warm climate and proximity to infrastructure. They are two completely different assets, but their comparison showcases San Albino's small-scale and impressive resource grade.

With a resource estimate of approximately 250,000 ounces of gold and a production volume of ~40,000 ounces in 2024, San Albino has a reserve-life index just above 6.0 years. In May 2025, Mako announced some interesting drill holes following exploration work on adjacent properties, including 39.15 g/t Au and 27.8 g/t Ag over 8.0 m and 18.14 g/t Au and 9.5 g/t Ag over 7.0 m.

How long will San Albino remain in production? Nobody can truly know, but I think Mako is likely to find additional milling material before the known resources are exhausted. If everything goes well, could Mako continue to report impressive margins from San Albino for another 5+ years? Yes, probably. After all, considering that commercial production at San Albino started in July 2021, it is still a relatively young asset.

As illustrated below, San Albino was brought to production following a relatively low amount of drilling of approximately 100,000 meters. With healthy cash flows generated from the ongoing operations, Mako is now in a much better position to deploy capital toward regional exploration. Indeed, San Albino is a relatively small asset, but producing ~40,000 ounces of gold per year at all-in sustaining costs below $1,400 per ounce provides the perfect platform for future growth. Mako's strategy is in sharp contrast to some of the junior explorers I have researched over the last few weeks. Without going into too much detail, I can mention Amex Exploration (AMX:CA) doing more than 600,000 meters of drilling without generating any form of revenue yet. In those cases, shareholder dilution unfortunately becomes the name of the game.

In a way, Mako Mining reminds me of Aya Gold & Silver (AYA:CA), a small silver producer I researched last month. As a reminder, Aya's flagship asset is the Zgounder pure-play silver mine located in Morocco. After reaching commercial production in December 2024, Aya now has a stable platform to support further growth. The operational team at Zgounder still has a lot of work to do on bringing operating costs lower, although the Feasibility Study was talking about an all-in sustaining cost of ~$10.00 per ounce of silver.

My 'Hold' rating reflects my optimism regarding Zgounder's free cash flow generation, but also my concerns about the reinvestment risks associated with the Boumadine Project.

With Zgounder's cash flows, Aya is reinvesting in regional exploration and advancing the Boumadine Project. In a completely different environment, Mako is employing the same strategy with its highly profitable San Albino Mine. As a top priority, capital is deployed into regional exploration to sustain production and cash flows, with the balance allocated to growth.

In my view, this is an attractive strategy as it avoids the serial dilution associated with pure-play exploration companies. As a result, Mako can grow on an organic basis without the need for constant capital injections. Of note, Mako purchased $4.7 million worth of shares as part of its buyback program in 2024. Purchases were not in substantial amounts, but it highlights the fact that Mako is generating excess liquidity instead of consuming it. There is no question that it creates a lot more room for error on the exploration front.

As indicated below, San Albino generated cash flows at the mine level of almost $55 million, after subtracting production costs of $38.2 million from total revenues of $91.6 million. With an average realized gold price of $2,361 throughout 2024, San Albino's cash-flow generation should increase quite meaningfully in the coming quarters. For a company with a ~$300 million market capitalization, free cash flows are about to become substantial, meaning that Mako's net cash balance of $5.0 million could grow much further.

Administrative Expenses & Executive Compensation

Another element that caught my attention on the annual income statement is the $8.6 million in general and administrative expenses, which is pretty good for a publicly-listed entity. To put this number in perspective, Aya Gold & Silver disclosed general and administrative expenses of $16.5 million over the same period. Indeed, Aya Gold & Silver is almost 5x larger based on market capitalization, but does it justify multi-million compensation packages for key management members?

As illustrated by the two compensation tables below, Mako's chief executive officer received a total compensation slightly above $1.0 million in 2024 compared to more than $6.6 million for Aya's CEO, Benoit Lasalle. For the key person running the show, we are talking about a compensation gap of 6.6x, and the difference becomes even larger as we go down the executive ranks. For example, Mako's CFO earned a total compensation of $440,000 in 2024 compared to more than $4.4 million for Aya's CFO.

How can we explain such a large difference in executive compensation? Does it mean that Mako is underpaying its management members, or is it the opposite, with Aya's management siphoning excessive value from shareholders? Everyone will have to answer this question for themselves, but in Mako's case, there is probably some discipline imposed by Wexford Capital, the company's largest shareholder with a 48% ownership interest. Additionally, Mako's CEO, Akiba Leisman, serves as a consultant for Wexford, indicating a close relationship between these two entities. In Aya's case, there is no controlling interest that could force some discipline on management compensation and overall administrative expenses. Of course, it is not the function of passive mutual funds and ETFs to enforce discipline.

To use an expression employed by a reader, the best way to make money with Aya is probably to become the CEO or a key executive. Mako seems to be much leaner, which is something I appreciate from the viewpoint of a prospective investor. At the end of the day, the only way I can make money as a minority shareholder is through a higher share price or dividend payments. Unfortunately, I am unlikely ever to receive millions worth of cheap stock options, so I must deploy my capital carefully and opportunistically.

After three years living alone in the woods, I now understand the energizing aspect of maintaining a lean cost structure and lifestyle. My lifestyle was a lot more bloated during my time in Toronto, and my mind was not as sharp. For a CEO with a multi-million-dollar package, where is the motivation to do anything outside of the box? After all, the share price would not matter very much at all.

Moss Mine Acquisition

In March 2024, Mako announced the completion of the Moss Acquisition, a small open-pit mine located in Arizona. Mako paid an aggregate purchase price of $6.5 million. After including interim production and the release of environmental bonds, Mako's net cash acquisition cost amounted to approximately $2.0 million, which represents just a few weeks' worth of cash flows from the San Albino Mine. Moss is already generating revenues, and Mako's management expects to improve asset performance over the coming quarters.

The acquisition of the producing Moss gold mine in Arizona, after the elimination of over US$ 60 million of existing liabilities associated with the mine, for a base purchase price of US$ 4.9 million, further reduced by US$ 1.5 million through the release of certain collateral, funded out of a fraction of this quarter’s corporate cash generation, is a testament to the powerful platform Mako has at its disposal. December 2024 Press Release

Given the price paid, the infrastructure already in place, the existing production, and the exploration potential, what is the downside for Mako's shareholders? Although Mako does not explicitly mention it, the offset likely includes environmental liabilities and reclamation costs. If it is indeed the case, compelling returns on invested capital are likely to be generated only if production can be maintained as far out into the future as possible. Expanding mine life is the best way to reduce the present value of those reclamation and rehabilitation costs.

In my view, paying a net cash consideration of only $2.0 million for Moss out of a bankruptcy proceeding is an indication of smart capital allocation. The risk/reward proposition of this transaction seems to be highly favourable to Mako's shareholders. After all, Moss demonstrated an ability to sustain production levels at ~30,000 ounces of gold per year since commercial production was declared in September 2018. As a reminder, San Albino produced ~40,000 ounces of gold in 2024.

According to the most recent Technical Report with an effective date of July 2021, the Moss Mine was estimated to contain approximately 490,000 ounces of gold at an average grade of 0.39 g/t. Although the head grade may look low at first sight, it is not atypical for heap leach operations in the region. It is also interesting to note that while Moss produces ~25% fewer ounces than San Albino, it is almost twice as big based on measured and indicated ounces. The future will tell, but I think Moss is a smart move coming from Mako's management team.

Eagle Mountain Project

In March 2024, Mako announced the acquisition of Goldsource Mines, owner of the Eagle Mountain Project located approximately 200 kilometres away from Georgetown, the capital of Guyana. Based on Mako's current share price of $3.90, the 13.2 million shares issued as part of the transaction are now worth slightly over $50 million. On a pro forma basis, Goldsource's shareholders owned approximately 17% of the combined entity.

In the oil and gas industry, the exploration success of Exxon Mobil (XOM) offshore Guyana has become legendary, following the announcement of the Liza discovery in May 2015. Since then, Exxon has announced major discoveries after major discoveries, and the company now expects to produce in excess of 1.3 million barrels of oil per day from Guyana by the end of 2027.

While the oil and gas industry is doing exceedingly well in the country, pretty much the same thing can be said about the local mining industry. As key players in the country, we find G Mining Ventures (GMIN:CA) with the Oko West Project, G2 Goldfields (GTWO:CA) with the OMZ Deposit, Omai Gold Mines (OMG:CA) with the Wenot Deposit and also Aris Mining (ARIS:CA) with its Toroparu Project. Over time, I plan to research all of those companies, starting probably with Aris Mining, given its close proximity to Collective Mining (CNL), a company I covered a few days ago.

If we return to Eagle Mountain, a Preliminary Economic Assessment on the project was published in March 2024. The report highlighted a resource base of approximately 1.2 million ounces of measured and indicated gold at an average grade of 1.2 g/t. The project also demonstrated its potential to deliver an attractive economic return, with an estimated internal rate of return of ~57% using a gold price of $1,850 per ounce. Similar to San Albino in Nicaragua and Moss in Arizona, Eagle Mountain would be mined through shallow open pit operations with an anticipated mine life of 15 years.

It is still early days for Eagle Mountain, but with initial capital costs estimated at $142 million and significant free cash flows coming from San Albino, this is an asset that Mako could put into production organically, without the need to issue additional shares. In this case, Mako's portfolio could grow from one producing asset as of the end of 2024 to three producing assets potentially by the end of the decade.

Conclusion

The San Albino mine forms the core pillar of Mako's organically funded growth strategy. Operational efficiency should improve in the next few quarters at the newly acquired Moss Mine in Arizona. Once the optimization is over, Mako will find itself with two cash-flowing assets and a development project in Guyana.

To be honest, I am quite impressed by Mako's strategy. If everything goes according to plan, which is a big if in the mining industry, Mako's production could surpass 100,000 ounces of gold per year somewhere around 2028. Considering the production volumes slightly below 40,000 ounces of gold in 2024, it would represent substantial growth rates.

My 'Buy' rating does not mean that I will rush to buy the stock on Monday morning. In fact, it simply means that at a certain price, this is an asset that I would like to own over the medium to long term. Along with gold prices, Mako's share price has rallied quite significantly since the start of 2025, and before initiating a new position, I would prefer to wait for some form of technical pullback first. Using current gold prices of almost $3,500, Mako looks cheap based on pretty much any valuation multiple. For example, in terms of free cash flow, we could easily be talking about a yield north of 20% in 2025, and potentially much higher if gold can maintain some of its recent gains. However, one element to keep in mind when thinking about Mako's valuation multiples is the relatively low reserve-life index, which is generally associated with lower multiples.

To deepen my understanding of the gold mining industry in Nicaragua, I will now turn my focus to Calibre Mining (CXB:CA), one of the largest producers in the country. As mentioned in other articles, if you would enjoy coverage on a specific miner, please leave a comment below, as I may fast-track the most popular requests.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UWM Holdings: Collect A 9.6% Dividend Yield While Waiting For Housing Market Recovery
data.description:
I have been following UWM Holdings (NYSE:UWMC) for quite a few years now, as its story fascinates me. The company, which operates as United Wholesale Mortgage, came public via a massive SPAC deal back in 2021. That deal, like many SPACs, suffered from unfortunate timing and shares traded down almost immediately once the transaction closed.

Years later, UWMC has struggled to regain the initial $10 transaction price, with the stock heading back under $5 recently:

Unlike many SPACs of that era, however, UWM is a large and profitable operation. I see it as only a matter of time until UWM turns the corner and starts to deliver material value for its shareholders.

The fact is that the company's growth over the years has been extremely impressive. It retains a motivated owner/operator who has a huge position in the company and is making shrewd moves. UWM has continued to invest through the housing cycle and is maintaining its capability for underwriting mortgages even at a time when demand is down and competitors are pulling back.

As a result, when the U.S. housing market gets back to more favorable conditions, UWM should be positioned as one of the leaders in the recovery.

The Recovery Has Been Delayed

Unfortunately for UWMC stockholders, however, that anticipated rebound in mortgage underwriting activity simply hasn't happened yet.

The primary reason why shouldn't be too surprising. The key obstacle here is that interest rates have remained higher for longer.

Here is a chart of the past two decades of average 30-year U.S. mortgage rates:

U.S. mortgage rates remain near their highest rates in a generation. A large number of potential homebuyers became accustomed to the 4% mortgages that were readily on offer throughout the 2010s. Some lucky folks obtained even cheaper mortgages in 2021. Now, however, the going rate is closer to 7%. Many potential homebuyers are simply going to keep renting until mortgages become more affordable again. High rates have also hurt the market in the sense that people with low rates on their existing homes are unwilling to move since any mortgage they obtained on a new house would be so much more costly than their existing loan.

On a related note, the fact that interest rates remain high means that refinancing activity is in the doldrums. If rates were to drop, there would be more refinancing activity in general, and presumably an uptick in cash-out refinancings as well.

UWM has also continued to invest heavily in building its distribution capabilities along with new technology. CEO Mat Ishbia does not apologize for this contrarian bet to keep investing in its broker channel during an industry slump. Here's Ishbia on the most recent conference call:

"Regardless of how the industry or markets may change due to technology rates, we will continue to lead the way. Since 2022, the mortgage broker channel share of the industry is up almost 40%, from about 19.7% to almost 28%, the highest level we've seen since 2008, I believe. This is incredible growth that we are very excited about.

I say this a lot, but I would ask all of you to go back and listen to our earnings call from [...] 2023 when we talk about game on pricing and how we are going to invest in the business and invest in the channel to grow. Many people were concerned about lowering the margins like we did. However, I knew and we knew it would be best for the broker channel long term.

The current numbers proved that we made the right decision and we're just getting started with all the success from that decision years ago."

UWM has maintained its mortgage underwriting and distribution capabilities in order to be ready when demand comes back. The company's submission to close rate is a mere 12.7 days, for example, whereas many industry peers are around 40 days. UWM is able to maintain this lightning-fast loan execution even during periods of high demand, with Ishbia noting that the company is able to double its daily loan production figures without sacrificing quality. Ishbia also sees something along the order of $70 million in future annualized cost savings related to the company's latest technology investments.

Given how depressed the mortgage market has been, it'd be easy for a company like UWM to go on defense and just reduce overhead while riding out the storm. Instead, UWM is actively improving its business and building a larger competitive moat for itself when times do start to get better.

Despite the continued investments, UWM has remained surprisingly profitable, even as revenues remain far below peak levels. Consider this chart of UWM Holdings' revenues:

It's quite impressive for a company to see its revenues fall by half from peak levels and still be able to generate profits for shareholders.

Analysts see the company producing 23 cents a share of earnings this year, which would put the stock around 18x this year's earnings. Not bad given the industry slump. And that would move to a single digit P/E ratio over the next two years, assuming the housing market starts to meaningfully recover as analysts currently model:

Is UWMC Stock Cheap Enough Given Ongoing Market Weakness?

I have been a skeptic on U.S. housing stocks over the past year. It seems that industry analysts had tried to remain fairly upbeat in terms of hoping for a quick turnaround in the sector. There's also been a lot of discussion around how shortages of available homes combined with demographic factors would support the market even in the face of higher rates.

However, this optimism now appears to be fading. Over the past six months, we've seen names such as the homebuilders and housing supply companies really sell off.

UWMC stock has been no exception. Shares are now down by about half from the 52-week highs:

I think that has pushed the stock well into value territory, even if housing activity remains subdued for the next year or two. The company is still meaningfully profitable even at today's reduced level of activity. And while it's taking longer than expected, at some point interest rates will come down again. On the plus side, as interest rates stay up here, that is creating more supply of higher interest mortgages which will get refinanced at the first whiff of lower rates. Thus, high rates are creating future refinancing demand for UWM.

I see UWM as being ideally situated. CEO Mat Ishbia is a talented capital allocator, and he's made good decisions throughout this downturn that have straddled the line between keeping UWM profitable while also reinvesting in the business so it can take market share as the industry recovers. I believe UWM is better run than many of its rival companies. While that difference hasn't really mattered to the share price yet, that's because the industry is still in a cyclical slump.

UWM is making technology investments now, which will allow it to be the industry leader when mortgage underwriting returns to being a growth business.

UWMC Stock Verdict

The dividend is another key component of the story here. UWM is paying out 40 cents per share annually in dividends, which amounts to a 9.6% dividend yield at the current price. That payout is well above the company's estimated 23 cent profit for fiscal year 2025. So, is there a problem there?

Do note that because the majority shareholders receive proportional distributions rather than cash dividends; in fact, UWM is only paying about $46 million a year in cash dividends. And CEO Mat Ishbia has reiterated his commitment to the 10 cent per quarter dividend on several conference calls. So, despite the apparently high payout ratio, the company can seemingly afford to pay that level of cash dividend even if profits remain subdued for the time being.

The 9.6% dividend yield rewards investors for their patience while waiting for the housing market to turn around. And, when that turn comes, UWM should be one of the leaders in growing the market thanks to its robust investments in mortgage underwriting technology.

I haven't taken a position in UWMC stock yet, as I keep waiting for investors to capitulate on the housing sector entirely. But we're seemingly getting closer to that point. Shares already look like a buy, particularly for income-focused investors. And if the stock gets much below $4 on further interest rate jitters, I'll be looking to establish a position personally.

data.title: Permian Resources Expands Smartly While Competitors Hold Back
data.description:
In the desert of West Texas, Permian Resources (NYSE:PR) is steadily building an impressive oil company that can stand out in a volatile market. What started as a merger in 2022 has now grown into one of the most efficient producers in the heart of the US shale belt. The recent numbers confirm what investors have been seeing: PR is posting record free cash flow, structurally reducing costs, and using that headroom wisely, not for blind volume but for careful returns. While other oil companies are reluctant to buy back shares during market downturns, PR is seizing attractive acquisition deals.

Permian Resources’ stock price has rallied in early 2025 on strong quarterly results and a smart acquisition in New Mexico, but it’s still well below what its underlying performance would warrant. What makes this stock worth buying, in my opinion, is its combination of low costs, healthy growth, generous cash flow, and shareholder-focused management. And if the market turns back to energy, PR could benefit strongly.

Why PR Is Stronger Than You Think

There are several forces working in Permian Resources’ favor right now. The oil price appears to be stabilizing around $70, which may cause concern for companies with high break-even costs, but is perfectly manageable for PR. According to management, the average breakeven level for new wells is around $30 per barrel. That’s exceptionally low, even in the Permian, and explains why the company can generate similar free cash flow at $60 WTI as it did last year at $75. That’s not an empty promise: PR posted a record $460 million in free cash flow in the first quarter of 2025. And that’s with oil prices lower than they were in the fourth quarter on average.

The stronger numbers aren’t a coincidence. Permian Resources cut its drilling costs again, now sitting at around $750 per lateral foot, down 8% from a year ago. At the same time, operating costs per barrel of oil equivalent fell to $7.54, which includes leases, transportation and overhead. Few US producers can stay below that threshold. And growth continues: production rose to 373,000 boe/d in Q1, of which 175,000 barrels were oil. These volumes are at the top end of the company’s own annual forecast, while the number of new wells for 2025 has actually been reduced from 285 to 275. Less capex, more output, it’s the kind of operating leverage you rarely see in a mid-cap E&P.

But what really stands out to me is the mentality of this management. While many peers were becoming cautious in April 2025 after a dip in the oil price, PR seized its chance. It bought back 4.1 million of its own shares at an average of $10.52. At the same time, it acquired APA Corporation’s non-core New Mexico assets: 13,320 acres in the heart of the Delaware Basin, adjacent to existing fields. The acquisition contributes 12,000 boe/d of low-decline production, over 100 high-quality drilling locations and an above-average Net Revenue Interest of 83%, enhancing returns per barrel. With a purchase price of $608 million in cash, or approximately $12,500 per net acre, the deal is attractively priced and immediately accretive to cash flow.

How PR Sets Itself Apart From Its Big Industry Peers

In a market where competition is fierce, it remains remarkable how Permian Resources is able to compete with names such as Diamondback, Devon and Ovintiv. Where these companies are often larger in absolute production, PR is smaller but more agile. Diamondback, for example, has economies of scale, but PR is at the same cost levels. Devon is more active in multiple regions, which spreads risks but also introduces inefficiencies. Ovintiv recently struggled with write-downs on gas reserves in Canada, while PR focuses entirely on oil and NGLs with low decline rates and little exposure to gas prices.

What also makes PR unique is its pure focus on the Delaware Basin. No distractions, no portfolio mix. Every barrel, every dollar is used in one region. And that focus is reflected in the results: well performance is improving quarter-on-quarter, the integration of previous acquisitions (such as Earthstone) went seamlessly and the new acquisition fits directly into existing units. Profit margins are not only high, but they are sustainable. In addition, the balance sheet is robust. Net debt is around 0.8x EBITDA, which is well below the industry average. The cash position is $702 million and total liquidity is even $3.2 billion. And the company is actively paying down expensive debt, earlier this year it prepaid $175 million of 9.875% loans. As a result, interest expenses are reduced by $17 million per year. For an E&P company of this scale, that is impressive.

Dividends and Valuation

If you invest in energy companies for income, PR is a good choice. It pays out $0.15 per share per quarter (or a dividend yield of around 5%). And that’s without the dependence on variable payouts like Devon. The payout ratio is around 28% of free cash flow, which means the dividend is sustainable even at lower oil prices. There is room to increase this over time, or to buy back shares when prices are low.

The valuation is hard to ignore. PR trades at less than 8x expected earnings and around 2.4x EV/EBITDA. That’s up to 40% lower than comparable Permian players. The stock is also cheap on a free cash flow basis: with an annual forecast of ~$1.4 billion FCF, that yields around 16% on the current market cap.

The PEG ratio – the P/E ratio divided by expected earnings growth is ~0.22. In other words, the growth is not yet priced in. This is rare for a company with such strong fundamentals and a proven track record in cost control, acquisitions, and shareholder value.

Risks To Mention

No investment is without risk. For PR, these risks include oil price volatility, operational integration of acquisitions, and potential ESG-related regulations. However, the company’s low breakeven prices, strong cash position and proven ability to smoothly integrate acquisitions make these risks manageable. Permian Resources has the buffers and flexibility to continue paying its dividend and delivering the operational performance we’ve seen to date in a more challenging macro environment.

Conclusion

Permian Resources is a company with discipline, vision, and cash flow. Where many competitors base their strategy on “hoping that oil stays high,” PR has established its foundations so solidly that it does not have to make concessions on growth, yield, or distribution even at $60 WTI. In fact, it actively invests when others are waiting.

The acquisition of APA’s assets is an example of this countercyclical thinking. The buybacks at low prices are a second. The combination of strong well performance, low costs, healthy balance sheet, smart allocation and attractive valuation makes this, in my eyes, one of the most attractive mid-cap oil stocks on the stock market. My view is therefore a Strong Buy.

data.title: Nexus Industrial: 8.6% Yielder Reenters Buy Zone
data.description:
Note: All amounts presented are in Canadian Dollars. All references are to the stock price on TSX and not the OTC symbol.

On our previous coverage of Nexus Industrial REIT (TSX:NXR.UN:CA) (OTC:EFRTF), we stayed on the sidelines with a "hold" rating. That was a second consecutive "hold" rating, as the risk-reward was slightly less than appealing.

We have not missed out by not going green on this one.

This was primarily a valuation call and one that we are now ready to reverse. We go over the Q1-2025 numbers and tell you why this is appealing.

Q1-2025

Nexus has almost completed its journey towards becoming a pure-play industrial REIT. At the end of Q1-2025, 99% of net operating income came from this segment, with the remaining originating from 3 tiny assets. These 3 should also exit the portfolio, though that is not necessary for our bull thesis.

What sits at the core of our bull thesis is the diversified industrial property portfolio across Canada.

In most REITs, you tend to see Ontario carry the bulk of the asset base. That is true here as well, but it is nice to see substantial assets across multiple provinces. The portfolio did deliver with a 6.6% net operating income (NOI) growth year over year. The funds from operations (FFO) growth was even more impressive at 12%.

Investors are probably still concerned about the one number that does not appear in the picture above. The adjusted funds from operations (AFFO). The AFFO came in at 15.3 cents a share (diluted) and the AFFO payout ratio was at 104.6%. As we had previously mentioned, we could not care less if the distribution was slashed in half. We only care about the total return when buying or owning shares. But beyond that, we see that there is room to grow into this distribution, as they have been doing for the last few quarters. It has been a bit of a bumpy road with portfolio changes and new vacancies. But the trend has been for clear improvement in this ratio. As a comparative, last year for Q1-2024, this was at 118%. The future remains bright for this metric as well. Management confirmed the same property NOI growth outlook this quarter. The rental spreads, while moderating, still remain a spectacular tailwind for the company.

These spreads are balanced with the right amount of leases coming off for renewal.

While some investors might love to see the entire portfolio repriced higher by 22%, we actually prefer REITs that create stability through good times and bad. We are actually seeing a test of this portfolio quality right now as there have been some large bankruptcies/vacancies for Nexus. Grace Road Lines and Peavey Mart were two major tenants that will be moving out due to bankruptcies. The two will add almost 450,000 square feet of additional vacancies across 3 major cities (Red Deer, London, and Calgary). The good part is that these rents were so far below the market that they represent some significant upside in the medium term.

And given the property had a long term lease well below market at approximately $4 net, re-leasing this property will be positive for us. We have strong tenant interest from three different groups as we speak and hope to have an offer signed in the next few weeks. The second tenant, Grace Road Lines occupies the site at 102 Avenue in Southeast Calgary but is anticipated to vacate either the end of May or June. Similar to London, we should be able to find a new tenant quickly. We are currently marketing it and have strong interest from a group, which hopefully will mitigate any impact to Nexus. We will proactively to minimize any downtime on these sites. Due to our solid operational performance, I continue to expect that we will achieve mid single digit industrial same property NOI growth for the year. In summary, we continue to advance our strategy in 2025 as Canada focused pure play industrial REIT.

Source: Nexus Q1-2025 Results Conference Call Transcript

We think this should resolve favorably as management expects, but we will be watching this closely.

One other favorable aspect for Nexus is their debt structure. We have seen many REITs blow through their NOI growth targets and then show negative year over year numbers for FFO and AFFO. That came via an undisciplined debt structure. Nexus, while not the best in the business, has kept a good profile with a weighted average debt maturity of 5.4 years.

The 2026 maturities have the lowest interest rate, so that bump up might be a bit painful. It is also the largest single year maturity. But based on NOI growth rates, the REIT remains poised to power through it.

Outlook & Verdict

The REIT is trading at around 10X FFO and at a substantial discount to its IFRS NAV. While it rings in cheap, we have to mention here that there is really no shortage of bargains in the industrial REIT space. Granite REIT (GRT.UN:CA) and Dream Industrial REIT (DIR.UN:CA) are also fairly cheap.

If you consider the relative size of those two (how big they are compared to Nexus), you should not balk at paying a little extra for those two. That's been the case with us as well. We have added a lot more of Granite REIT and not added to our Nexus position.

While we are seeing Nexus as cheap but not outstandingly cheap, some insiders are not in the same boat.

Edwin McLaughlin has added about 100,000 units via public market purchases this year. There were many more purchases last year. He owns an industrial warehousing company and probably understands the value in these units more than most. This does add to the bull thesis.

Nexus is also poised to obtain an investment grade rating within a year as per the company. This might put it in more investment screens and help with giving it the attention it deserves. The stock is now at $7.38, and previously we had held out for an "under $7.75" as a buy point. We have it now, and we will be stamping a "buy" rating once again.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Aviva: Upside To Consensus As We Wait Direct Line Acquisition
data.description:
Aviva’s (OTCPK:AIVAF) (OTCPK:AVVIY) proposed acquisition of Direct Line holds the potential to enhance shareholder value, with management projecting an EPS uplift of approximately 15% within two years of completion. Since our last update (December 2024), the company's total return has been >40% (Fig. 1), thanks to stronger-than-expected results, and we have reached our desired target price. As a reminder, Aviva combines more than 10% operating EPS growth with a 7% increase in the dividend per share, offering a compelling total yield close to 10%. Here at the Lab, our standalone buy thesis was underpinned by three key factors: 1) the group’s geographic diversification, 2) upside potential from individual annuities and CSM expansion, and 3) a robust Solvency II capital position. Compared to L&G, the company disclosed its Q1 results update, and Aviva's trading performance was mixed.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

Starting with a quick overview of Aviva's Q1 trading update, we report that life retirement solutions beat our expectations, with a BPA volume of £1.3 billion. Following the launch of a new product, the company's equity release sales also showed growth. Aviva reported a positive inflow from the wealth as well. On a negative note, workplace flow was down in Q1 2025 due to the exit of a large scheme. Regarding general insurance, the combined operating ratio was 1.5 basis points higher, which appears to be driven by Storm Eowyn, the largest windstorm in 40 years. Growth was better than expected, and the Solvency II ratio was aligned with a 201% output (vs. the expectation of 200%). The relevant change was due to the dividend payment, which was partially offset by the company's capital generation. Still related to the balance sheet, Aviva's liquidity reached £1.8 billion, with a leverage ratio of 31.9% from 28.9% in Fiscal Year 2024.

Source: Aviva Q1 trading update - Fig 2

Why are we still Positive?

  1. Health & Protection volumes jumped 32% on a yearly basis. This was boosted by the inclusion of the AIG protection book since April 2024. Aviva's margins have improved sequentially for four consecutive quarters. This was also supported by annuities sales of £1.76 billion. As expected, annuities are growing, supported by favorable UK legislation, and Aviva continues to execute its strategy effectively, delivering sustainable growth. Following the Q1 results update, it is crucial to report the CEO's latest words: "Aviva has got off to a great start in 2025. We continue to trade strongly." This reinforces our conviction to increase the company's target;
  2. There was no update to the 2025 financial targets, but there is a positive outlook for the year. The company reported a COR expected to decline over 2025, with operating profit targets for Health and Wealth reiterated. The Direct Line acquisition is reportedly "firmly on track", with a competition authority investigation deadline of 10 July, and reformed targets expected following the completion of the deal. Having said that, on a standalone basis, as per the FY 2024 results (Fig. 3), the company confirmed an EBIT of £2 billion for 2026 and cumulative cash remittances of over £5.8 billion. We believe many sell-side analysts have yet to reflect the potential earnings accretion from the proposed Direct Line acquisition, as they await regulatory clarity on the deal’s approval;

  3. On a positive note, the holding company's cash was surprisingly strong, even excluding cash remitted to the Group to fund the Direct Line acquisition. The company will remain in a highly healthy position even after paying for the cash component. This is well-supported by its standalone business and its essential role in generating cash flow.

Source: Aviva FY 2024 results ppt - Fig 3

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Here at the Lab, we have increased our EPS projections for Aviva by approximately 8% to 10% over the 2025–2028 period following its Q1 2025 update. The following factors primarily drive the upward revisions: we upgraded our Insurance, Wealth, and Retirement forecasts to reflect stronger-than-expected growth, particularly within the wealth and protection segments. Sales momentum in both areas exceeded our expectations, supported by healthy net inflows across workplace pensions and the retail investment platform. As a result, we now project the 2027 wealth operating profit to better align with Aviva’s target of £280 million. We have also revised our general insurance outlook upward due to better-than-expected underlying underwriting performance, despite Storm Eowyn resulting in higher claims. Indeed, even after adjusting for weather effects, underlying profitability in the non-life segment appears slightly ahead of our original assumptions. Management commentary also reinforced the company’s discipline in maintaining pricing adequacy across both personal and commercial lines. We value Aviva with 2026 estimates, and our updated EPS forecasts remain significantly above Wall Street consensus by approximately 15% for 2025–2027. We attribute this gap to persistent market skepticism, particularly regarding Aviva’s ability to meet key IWR division targets. Going to the P&L, and including the Direct Line acquisition, we project a core operating profit of £2.85 billion, resulting in a net profit of approximately £2 billion, with an EPS of 63.8 pence.

Continuing to value the company at a 10x P/E target (Fig. 4), which aligns with the sector median (and L&G valuation), our target price is £6.38 per share. Considering a 7% dividend yield, we anticipate a total return of more than 13% over the next 12 months.

Fig 4

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) execution risks on Direct Line acquisition, which represents almost a 15% upside on EPS revision, 2) Aviva’s inability to meet key IWR division targets, 3) volatile equity market with credit risks and default rates, 4) longevity risks, 5) higher combined ratio evolution (as happened with the Irish storm in Q1 2025), 6) any change in distribution, even if the company has sufficient cash flow generation for M&A.

Conclusion

The company's business mix and risk profile have improved significantly under its current CEO, and we continue to find Aviva attractive in terms of valuation and conservative consensus expectations. For this reason, we reiterate our overweight rating, raising our target price to £6.38.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PSI May Not Fit The Future Of Chip Stocks
data.description:
Let me get straight to the point: in my opinion, the Invesco Semiconductors ETF (NYSEARCA:PSI), which passively tracks an index built on an asset allocation logic different from the classic market weight, will not be able to accurately represent the semiconductor equity segment.

The reason? The sector’s market cap narrowing process is irreversible. I’ll explain more in a moment, but first…

Do you really understand what PSI is?

PSI is a passive ETF that minimizes tracking error by following the Dynamic Semiconductor Intellidex Index, an index that prides itself on having a quantitative stock-picking structure behind it. The selection criteria are based on price momentum, earnings momentum, valuation, quality, and management action.

The result: 30 U.S. semiconductor companies selected from a universe of 2,000 U.S. stocks, using the FactSet Revere Hierarchy. These are split into two groups: large caps (top quintile), with 8 selected stocks that make up 40% of the total weight, and small/mid-caps (bottom 4 quintiles), with 22 stocks making up 60%. Large caps are each given a 5% weight in the index; the others, 2.73%. The index is rebalanced quarterly. And PSI passively follows this structure, with a 0.51% expense ratio.

The result

It’s impossible not to notice that the top three holdings are KLA Corp, Lam Research Corp, and Texas Instruments, companies we’re not exactly used to seeing in the top spots when talking about semiconductor ETFs. What’s more, there are significant omissions, like key international names (e.g., TSM, ASML).

And this is the peculiarity of PSI, something that must be deeply understood. The benchmark essentially applies a factor in a passive way, which has a disruptive impact on the fund’s asset allocation.

You need to know that

Since PSI follows pre-defined construction models, the first question that comes to mind is: how does it perform compared to a market-weighted ETF? My picks are SMH and SOXX. And the overall result is that PSI is, on average, underperforming. Or more precisely:

  • Excluding the last 5 years, PSI actually delivered very competitive returns, even compared to SMH, which is considered the top performer in the category. So, a backward-looking analysis done in 2020 would have likely labeled PSI as at least a “competitive” ETF.

  • But since 2020, it started to underperform, resulting in a 10-year underperformance compared to SMH.

The reason? With market cap becoming increasingly concentrated in fewer names, market-weighted ETFs have simply done a better job at representing the sector.

To avoid the same mistake we could’ve made in 2020, I now ask: from a forward-looking perspective, which market cap structure will best represent the sector going forward? I have reason to believe that PSI may continue to underperform, even looking ahead.

The real reason behind the underperformance

According to a McKinsey report, which I consider a must-read for anyone looking to ride the semiconductor wave, only 5% of companies generated nearly all the sector’s economic profit in 2024. Among them, of course: Nvidia, TSMC, Broadcom, and ASML.

And I ask myself: how can an ETF that assigns predefined weights to companies truly represent such a concentrated market? The answer? It simply can’t.

Let’s be clear: this doesn’t mean PSI will deliver negative returns. The sector shows a 19% ROE, and revenue is expected to grow at an average annual rate of 10% through 2030. But if you break down where that growth is coming from, it’s always the same winners, and PSI is unlikely to emphasize them.

AI-focused companies are projected to grow revenues by 18%, especially if U.S.-China relations remain stable. On the other hand, non-AI-focused companies, or those not tied to China, are expected to grow just 2–3%. That’s not bad, but it’s a completely different outlook.

And in a market this imbalanced, I believe market-weighted solutions do a better job representing the true dynamics.

Risk

On the other hand, market-weighted ETFs are going through an unusual phase: SMH has nearly 20% in NVDA, making it one of the most concentrated ETFs in the sector.

That is a risk, in the sense that, yes, the market is heavily concentrated in a few tickers, but that also increases concentration risk, no matter how strong today’s growth prospects may be.

We all know the future isn’t written, especially in a time of major political uncertainty like the one we’re in now.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe the right rating for PSI is SELL.

It’s not a SELL on the semiconductor sector itself, it’s a SELL on an ETF whose construction, in other contexts, I would have considered rewarding.

But in a vertical and highly concentrated market like semiconductors, its structure feels too diluted, and in my view, it’s likely to deliver less alpha than its peers.

data.title: British American Tobacco: Time To Take Profits?
data.description:
British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) (OTCPK:BTAFF) is one of the world’s largest tobacco companies, operating in over 180 countries. Although historically a combustible cigarette business, the company is increasingly diversifying its revenue mix into next generational products. These include vaping devices, heat-not-burn devices, and modern oral (a tobacco-free nicotine pouch). The US is the Group’s most important market and accounts for 45% of its revenue. The balance is made up of some developed markets, but predominantly consists of emerging markets. Despite having appreciated about 60% YoY in price and trading at a higher multiple compared to its historical average, the company is making smart investments in growing product categories and divesting from laggards, all against a favorable regulatory backdrop. For me, it's a Buy.

Why It's Pricier

British American Tobacco currently trades on a P/E of 10X. This reflects a material re-rating from the sub 6X P/E multiple an investor could buy the share on just two years ago. There have been stock-specific as well as macro-related factors driving this re-rating. From an operational perspective, management has been successful in de-gearing the balance sheet, with net debt to EBITDA now at 2.4X, having peaked above 4X in 2017. This has been driven by continued excellent cash generation, as well as the sale of a stake in their ITC associate business in India.

Moreover, management has demonstrated progress in Next Generational Products, with the category moving into profitable territory. A segment like Modern Oral, although small, is performing tremendously well and can move the needle on Group earnings when earnings are only growing in the low single digits.

On the macro front, a global flight to safety has resulted in tobacco stocks generally re-rating, and BATS has benefitted from this trend.

Perhaps most importantly, the company has continued to demonstrate exceptional cash generation ability. While there have been doubts in the past over the company’s ability to maintain the progressive dividend policy, these doubts have essentially been put to bed, thanks to strong operational cash flows, balance sheet de-gearing, and management’s willingness to monetize the ITC stake.

Although the dividend growth has slowed, it has remained progressive. The 10-year dividend per share compound annual growth rate is 5% (In GBP), and this falls to 3.3% over the past 3 years. Nevertheless, there was more than enough margin of safety in the valuation historically such that any dividend growth was not priced into the share. Current free cash flow per share of GBP3.5 implies a free cash flow yield of 10%. With cash generation expected to remain robust and a key feature of the investment case, this will be more than sufficient to maintain a progressive dividend (Current dividend yield is 7%), with some cash left over for buybacks and deleveraging.

Valuation Looking Full?

Given management guidance for low single digit earnings growth this year, moving to mid-single digit growth next year, a 10X P/E could be deemed a fair rating for the stock. This is particularly probably considering the myriad regulatory issues the company continues to face as well as the issues being faced in U.S. vaping, with illicit Chinese disposables gaining 70% share of the market.

A back of the envelope returns calculation would imply ~12% returns p.a., given a 7% dividend yield and assuming 5% growth in earnings per year, and no further re-rating in the stock’s multiple. This is reasonable, but not terribly exciting. However, I would argue investors should extend their horizon by a few years and consider British American Tobacco’s trajectory in 3–5 years’ time. There are the beginnings of some interesting developments which may boost BAT’s earnings trajectory in time.

Groundwork for More Growth

First, the Chinese illicit disposables issue. With illegal disposable vaping devices having garnered 70% of the market in the US, British American Tobacco stands to gain enormously as the number one legal player once the tide starts to turn against illegal disposables. This is already in the early stages of development. President Trump recently made several key appointments, including:

  • Robert Kennedy as Secretary for US Health and Human Services.
  • Marty Makary as FDA Commissioner.
  • Bret Koplow as acting CTP (Center for Tobacco Products).

Importantly, these positions were recently occupied by individuals that had demonstrated distrust of tobacco. Robert Kennedy has made several statements voicing support for the fight against illegal disposables. He has also been seen using modern oral products. His conduct thus far implies a more friendly demeanor towards British American Tobacco's products, tobacco and non-tobacco alike.

An executive order from the President banning disposable vapes is also a possibility, and would likely be the fastest and most effective measure in reducing illegal disposables in the market materially. Otherwise, increased inter-agency cooperation, more media attention and state by state regulation. Louisiana, for example, enacted legislation in 2023 requiring all manufacturers to register their products – this proved highly effective in combatting illicit disposables. Ten states have since followed suit. These are all the kinds of measures that will start to turn the tide against illicit disposables.

British American Tobacco generated £1.8 billion in revenue from vaping in 2025. Of this, £1 billion was from the U.S. This is 4% of Group revenue, and this is a crucial component of the revenue mix given positive high growth rates for the category, versus negative growth rates for U.S. combustibles. With value share of the legal market at 50%, this implies British American Tobacco has 15% share of the total market, including illicit. This means if the illegal market halved in share and British American Tobacco gained proportionally, market share could increase from 15% of the total market to 33%, thus almost a doubling of revenue from this category in the U.S. That’s 4 percentage points of additional revenue growth. Given management is only guiding for low single digit revenue growth, the boost could be enormously beneficial to earnings.

Modern Oral

Another potential growth catalyst is the Modern Oral category. Although small in the current mix, at approximately 3% of Group revenue, the growth rates here are impressive and potentially needle-moving. In FY 2024, its Modern Oral revenues were up 53% in constant currency organic terms. The 4.5-year CAGR since H1 2020 has been 48%. If this growth rate can be sustained, this category can add 1–2 percentage points to Group revenue per year in the next few years, and more as it increases in relative size. Their Velo 2.0 Modern Oral product has proved successful outside of the U.S. (leadership position in 21 European markets), and with a pending Premarket Tobacco Market Application (PMTA) approval in the U.S., British American Tobacco is poised to replicate its success in the U.S. market. Philip Morris' Zyn still dominates the US market; however, BAT is well positioned with Velo 2.0, which is a much improved product over the first iteration, to challenge the incumbent once PMTA approval is received.

Competition in this space is intense, however. In the US, the market is gate-kept by the FDA through the PMTA approval process. Philip Morris’ Zyn modern oral product accounts for 68% market share, with Altria’s On! Product in second position at 19% share. British American Tobacco’s Velo product currently only has 6% share. However, this is the first generation product and is widely regarded as vastly inferior to the likes of Zyn. Therein lies the opportunity once Velo 2.0 receives PMTA approval.

Valuation

My valuation methodology for British American Tobacco has historically been focused on dividends, as this has accounted for the bulk of investor returns for the stock. I expect re-rating to play a more important part in returns in the next few years, given the above catalysts mentioned. Using a 3-year dividend discount model with an exit P/E multiple of 12.4x in year 3, the stock is currently 15% undervalued. Bear in mind that historically, the company used to trade on a mid-teens multiple. With the business mix evolving for the better over the medium term into higher-growth categories, there is potential for British American Tobacco to begin to close the rating gap to a Philip Morris, for example, which trades on a PE of 23x. The risk is to the upside in my view.

Risks

Risks to watch out for which would necessitate changing this view include failure to secure PMTA approval in the US for Velo 2.0, as well as ineffective regulation or measures implemented against illegal Chinese disposables. Chinese exporters are likely to try to circumvent any measures, potentially round-tripping through other countries. Additionally, an eye should most certainly be kept on the US combustibles market, where volumes are falling 9% p.a. A further deterioration, which, I think, is unlikely, would impact the company’s cash flows. This remains an important component of British American Tobacco's earnings.

Final Thoughts

Overall, I think investors need to be careful when simply assuming management guidance for this year and next and extrapolating into the future. Management has missed guidance in the past to the downside, backtracking on their high single-digit earnings growth, and then mid-single-digit earnings growth guidance, to where we find ourselves today: low single-digit earnings growth. I would suggest it is possible the company may revise their guidance numbers upwards in time, given the potential catalysts highlighted above. This is likely a few years out, however. With the market being forward-looking, it would be wise not to try time when the potential good news is priced in. At the same time, the dividend yield remains attractive at 7%. I would be hesitant to take profits too quickly on British American Tobacco.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Oaktree Specialty Lending: Credit Issues Still A Problem
data.description:
Investors in Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (NASDAQ:OCSL) are still paying the price for an above average non-accrual ratio, as the BDC’s stock continues to sell at an above-average discount to net asset value.

The business development company, however, reset its dividend in the prior quarter and changed its dividend composition, which may lead to relief for the coverage ratio moving forward.

The new dividend consists of a base dividend, that has been realigned with the BDC’s lowered net investment income, and Oaktree Specialty Lending is going to pay supplemental dividends that correspond to its ability to produce excess net investment income (above what is needed to fund the base dividend).

High investment losses and higher than normal non-accruals in the credit portfolio forced the business development company to lower its dividend in 1Q25.

My last stock classification on Oaktree Specialty Lending was ‘Hold’ which I have to reaffirm since the BDC’s non-accruals got even worse in the March-quarter. Though the dividend pay-out trend might improve moving forward, the non-accrual issue is probably going to remain a drag on the BDC’s net investment income and NAV multiple.

Troubled Credit Portfolio Weighed Down By High Non-Accruals

Oaktree Specialty Lending is a specialty financing company that provides its portfolio companies with access to much-needed capital and that can’t easily access financing from risk-averse traditional banks.

As of March 31, 2025, the business development company had a total investment portfolio valued at $2.9 billion, with about 84 percent of all investments being allocated to First and Second Liens.

The core investment focus of Oaktree Specialty Lending is on the software and service business sector, which is characterized by highly predictable sales and income.

While Oaktree Specialty Lending did originate new investments in the last twelve months, the business development company unfortunately saw substantial net asset value pressure related to bad investments it made in the past.

In the last twelve months, Oaktree Specialty Lending reported cumulative total net realized and unrealized losses in the amount of $164.9 million and the business development company suffered losses in every single quarter in the last year, as shown by the yellow bar in the next chart.

The underlying problem for the business development company is credit quality which has taken a bad hit in the last year and, thus, Oaktree Specialty Lending’s non-accrual trend is not looking great, to put it mildly: The BDC is exhibiting a substantial deterioration in its credit quality over the last twelve months which caused the non-accrual ratio to tick up to 4.6% as of March 31, 2025, reflecting a YoY increase of 2.2 percentage point.

Non-accruals rose 0.7 percentage points QoQ and 10, instead of 9, companies were not performing.

Oaktree Specialty Lending’s non-accrual trend looks ugly:

Dividend Presently Fully Covered

The business development company is presently dealing with dividend pay-out metrics that are not great, but are getting better. Oaktree Specialty Lending earned $0.45 per share in adjusted net investment income in the March-quarter, which reflected a substantial 20% YoY decrease in income.

Because Oaktree Specialty Lending adjusted its dividend lower in the prior quarter, however, the business development company managed to cover its full dividend pay-out of $0.40 per share with net investment income.

For 2Q25, Oaktree Specialty Lending declared its base dividend of $0.40 per share, which is going to get supplemented by an extra payment of $0.02 per share as a means to distribute excess portfolio income. Both dividends are payable on June 30, 2025.

In terms of net investment income, a widely underperforming credit portfolio has obviously had a negative impact on Oaktree Specialty Lending’s results in 2Q25. The BDC suffered a YoY drop in its adjusted total investment income of 21% in the March-quarter to $77.2 million as a larger number of loans stopped producing income. This decrease in income has been anticipated by the business development company and has led to a 27% dividend cut.

Thanks to this dividend realignment, Oaktree Specialty Lending is more likely than not going to see healthier dividend pay-out metrics moving forward.

While OCSL might see a higher margin of safety as far as the dividend is concerned, the non-accrual situation is not yet resolved, which is why my stock classification for Oaktree Specialty Lending remains 'Hold.'

Discount To BV Appropriate Given High Non-Accruals

Oaktree Specialty Lending is obviously going through a rough patch right now, and passive income investors have paid the price here as well: The stock of Oaktree Specialty Lending has dramatically underperformed in the last year and lost about 26% of its value. As a consequence, the non-accrual issue has resulted in a distressed valuation or Oaktree Specialty Lending, which is likely appropriate given its much higher than normal non-accruals.

At the time of writing, the stock of Oaktree Specialty Lending is selling for a 13% discount to net asset value. Oaktree Specialty Lending is not the only business development company with a sub-optimal non-accrual ratio.

There are a couple of other business development companies that I have covered, such as TriplePoint Venture Growth BDC (TPVG), Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) and FS KKR Capital Corp (FSK) that all have higher than average non-accrual ratios and thus also sell at substantial discounts to net asset value.

In terms of Price-To-NII ratio, Oaktree Specialty Lending is selling for a valuation multiple 8% above the BDC peer group average ratio of 7.5x. Business development companies with weak credit profiles tend to sell for either big discounts to NAV or low Price-To-NII ratios, which is both the case here for Oaktree Specialty Lending as well as other BDCs working through their credit issues.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Not Be Bullish

The investment thesis for Oaktree Specialty Lending can turn bullish if Oaktree Specialty Lending gravitates toward healthier dividend pay-out metrics while keeping a lid on its non-accrual ratio.

Only in the event that the business development company can restore healthier credit quality will a re-rating to net asset value be possible. Anything short of it will most probably lead to a continued depressed valuation multiple for the business development company.

My Takeaway

Oaktree Specialty Lending is going through some serious pain and portfolio realignment issues which have been characterized by an increase in investment losses, both unrealized and realized, and an unfavorable non-accrual trend in its credit portfolio. That being said, I think that the pay-out trend is going to show promise with the dividend realignment and the discount to net asset value widely signals that investors understand the BDC’s credit issues.

Since the dividend has been restructured and realigned with lower net investment income expectations, Oaktree Specialty Lending should be able to see a rising margin of safety moving forward.

Only if OCSL also improves its non-accruals are we going to see a stock re-rating, in my view. And since the BDC’s non-accruals continued to rise in the last quarter, it may be quite a while until this happens. Hold.

data.title: This Is The Price I'd Buy Shopify
data.description:
Shopify (NASDAQ:SHOP) (TSX:SHOP:CA) is appearing to be a clear beneficiary of generative AI. The e-commerce landscape remains somewhat fragmented even as the story matures, but SHOP is shaping up to be a potential long-term consolidator as it has offered an intentionally completed product portfolio. The company maintains a net cash balance sheet and is solidly GAAP profitable. I expect the company to sustain aggressive top- and bottom-line growth for many years, but also acknowledge that the current valuation appears to already have priced this in. SHOP remains one of the highest quality names in my coverage, but the math just doesn’t work out here. This isn’t the time to chase returns or fall victim to the “fear of missing out.” Wait for better prices or look for cheaper alternatives elsewhere - I rate the stock with a neutral rating.

SHOP Stock Price

I last covered SHOP in March, where I recommended avoiding the stock due to the lofty valuation. The stock has declined by 1% since then, underperforming the broader market by around 5%.

The business is performing exemplarily, but the math is still difficult, as I worry that upgrading the stock here might simply be bull market capitulation.

SHOP Stock Key Metrics

Shopify is the most important enabler of e-commerce when retailers are not looking to support the Amazon (AMZN) death star. Kidding aside, SHOP does offer a fully comprehensive product suite to help retailers with their online businesses.

SHOP has been able to take so much market share in part due to its decision to outsource or partner for much of its functionality, enabling it to offer a more comprehensive product suite even if it is not owning the full profit picture.

The latest quarter saw the company deliver 27% YoY revenue growth to $2.4 billion, exceeding guidance for “mid-twenties” growth.

It is astounding that the company has continued to sustain such aggressive GMV and revenue growth, even as it already posted 20% or higher growth in 2023 and 2024.
